planting.note: Direct sow in fall or plant container-grown plants in fall or spring in very well-drained, dry, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; black sage is native to coastal sage scrub and chaparral of the California Coast Ranges and Channel Islands, adapted to the Mediterranean climate of California. Summer drought is essential — avoid irrigation in summer.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; black sage is one of the dominant shrubs of California coastal sage scrub — a white-flowered, strongly aromatic native sage with dark green, wrinkled, very fragrant leaves that produce a characteristic camphor-sage scent that perfumes the California coastal air on warm days. The clusters of small, white to pale lavender flowers in spring are exceptionally attractive to native bees — black sage is one of the most important native bee plants in California, producing abundant nectar and pollen particularly for bumble bees, carpenter bees, and many solitary native bees. The dried seed capsules (nutlets) are eaten by seed-eating birds and small mammals. Completely drought-tolerant once established, requiring no summer irrigation in coastal California gardens. The aromatic leaves can be used as a culinary sage substitute, with a flavor described as a blend of culinary sage and camphor. An essential native for California habitat gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, rocky or loamy soil in partial shade; hummingbird sage spreads by underground rhizomes to form dense, weed-suppressing groundcover colonies under oaks and in dappled shade, making it an excellent low-maintenance native California groundcover.

directions: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean soil in partial shade; hummingbird sage is one of the most useful native California salvias for gardens, tolerating more shade than most salvias while producing large, striking, rose-magenta flower spikes on tall stems — among the largest flowers of any native California sage, strongly attractive to Anna's hummingbirds and rufous hummingbirds throughout the blooming season from late winter through spring. The large, crinkled, gray-green aromatic leaves smell strongly of fresh pineapple and sage when crushed. Spreads by rhizomes to form gradually expanding colonies that provide excellent, drought-tolerant groundcover under California oaks and in dappled shade. One of the best California natives for replacing invasive English ivy under trees while maintaining shade coverage and providing outstanding wildlife value for hummingbirds and native bees.

planting.note: Plant in fall in very well-drained, dry, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; chaparral sage grows naturally on dry slopes and chaparral edges of the southern California Coast Ranges and Santa Barbara area. Completely summer drought tolerant — do not water in summer once established; overwatering causes rapid decline.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry, rocky or sandy, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; chaparral sage (purple sage) is a large, aromatic California native shrub of the southern Coast Ranges, producing distinctive white-woolly (leucophylla = white-leaved) leaves that reflect harsh summer sunlight and reduce water loss — an adaptation to the extreme summer heat and drought of California chaparral. The lavender-purple flowers in late spring to early summer are striking and produce abundant nectar for native bees and hummingbirds. One of the 'allelopathic' California sages — the plant releases volatile chemicals that inhibit the germination of competing plants around it, creating a distinctive bare zone between the sage canopy and the surrounding grassland. The famous 'elfin forest' scent of California chaparral comes largely from this species and coast sage (S. apiana). Essential for authentic California coastal sage scrub plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring in very well-drained, dry to average, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky, lean soil in full sun; golden chia grows naturally in dry, open chaparral margins, grasslands, and disturbed ground of California and the Southwest. In the wild it grows as a winter annual, germinating in fall after the first rains.

directions: Direct sow in fall or very early spring in very well-drained, dry, sandy or rocky, lean soil in full sun; golden chia is the native California and Southwest counterpart of the commercial chia (Salvia hispanica), producing a similar small, nutritious, edible seed in dense whorls on a single upright stem — the seeds were a staple food of Chumash, Diegueno, and other California Native peoples, eaten dry, mixed with water as a drink, or ground into a flour. The gelatinous coating of wet golden chia seeds, like commercial chia, creates a mucilaginous texture used in traditional beverages. The deep blue to violet flower whorls are attractive and pollinated by native bees. An important cultural and ecological plant of the California landscape — the name 'chia' refers to this native California species in traditional California usage, predating the commercial use of S. hispanica. Grows as a quick winter annual in the California climate cycle.

planting.note: Plant seeds in spring in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average soil in full sun; tough bumelia grows naturally in dry, sandy, open scrub and upland pine flatwoods of north-central Florida. Seeds benefit from scarification and warm stratification. A relatively rare Florida endemic.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average soil in full sun; tough bumelia (Alachua buckthorn) is a native evergreen shrub or small tree of north-central Florida's dry, sandy scrub and upland pine habitats — one of several Sideroxylon species native to Florida, related to the widespread tough buckthorn (S. tenax) of the Coastal Plain but with a more restricted distribution centered on Alachua County, Florida. Like other bumpelia/sideroxylon species, it produces leathery, elliptic, dark green leaves, small white flowers in clusters that attract pollinators, and small, oval, dark purple-black drupes that are eaten by mockingbirds, catbirds, and other fruit-eating birds. The spiny branches (typical of the genus) provide excellent nesting cover. An important but rarely cultivated native for the dry, sandy, acidic scrub gardens of north-central Florida.
